title: Compress this AI diligence storyline to its IC one-pager
guidance: Reward the version that helps a partner reach a buy/no-buy call. Penalise a version that summarises every section without a verdict or a foregrounded risk.

runner_input.instruction: Compress the storyline below into an IC one-pager: lead with the answer, then the few things that change the decision.
runner_input.constraints: Max 160 words. Title states the verdict. Foreground the one risk that could change the buy view. No new facts.

source_blocks.text: Project Saffron — Halden Standards Ltd owns the Halden Code, the de-facto UK standard for food-safety management schedules (cleaning, temperature, allergen and pest-control regimes), used by food manufacturers, hospitality groups and contract caterers. Aldgate Capital is considering a buyout. All figures synthetic.

Overall view: AI is a net positive. The moat is institutional, no funded challenger is attacking, and three AI levers compound the case. Underwrite a buy, gated on two diligence items — integration-contract content-control terms (defensive) and ML capability/roadmap ownership (offensive).

items: FY26A: ARR £11.2m; recurring billings £11.6m; total revenue £10.4m; gross margin 88%; Adj. EBITDA £3.1m (30%); FCF £2.4m
HaldenOS platform on 48% of ~900 clients, up from 22% a year ago; 18% ARR uplift on migrations
NRR 112%; GRR 91%; NPS +58; voluntary attrition 6.1%; blended ACV ~£12k
FY31F plan: recurring billings £39.5m (~3.4x; 28% CAGR); Adj. EBITDA £14.2m (36%)
Above-plan upside (CIM): migration 30% not 18% (+£1.4m EBITDA); pricing 8% not 4% (+£4.9m); churn 5% not 9% (+£2.6m)
1,180 ratified schedules across 90 process types; endorsed by the Institute of Food Safety Management (IFSM) with a 12-year exclusivity
Named in BRCGS audit guidance and contract-catering tenders; embedded in 14 QMS-software integrations
Buyers: food manufacturers 38% of ARR, hospitality & contract caterers 34%, retail & convenience 16%, other 12%
Why they buy: BRCGS/EHO audit assurance, allergen-liability protection under Natasha's Law, operational efficiency
AutoSchedule (AI asset/process-to-schedule mapping) shipped Jan 2026; £140k ARR by month 4; 88–95% accuracy on a 12,000-SKU test
Proprietary AI authoring tool used by the 8-FTE food-safety author team; an analytics module was pulled forward
Opportunity levers: 40–55% authorship efficiency; a compliance-scoring layer at 2–3x ARPU; an SME tier for ~120,000 small food businesses priced out today

source_blocks.text: The one credible AI-enabled disruption path is a QMS/food-safety software vendor (e.g. SafePlate, Kitchen Sentinel, Comply&Co) using LLMs to generate a competing schedule library, routing around the integration dependency, and bidding for trade-body and auditor recognition — five sequential steps over 5–8 years. Customer DIY fails at the auditor gate (needs a citable, ratified source) and the allergen-liability gate (Natasha's Law is criminally enforced).

CTO Daniel Mertens is praised across primary interviews and the team has shipped real AI, but the CIM org chart names 11 developers, 1 DevOps, 2 product and 3 QA across an 18-FTE engineering team — and no ML or data engineer. AutoSchedule's accuracy implies real ML plumbing exists; whether it is built in-house, rented from a vendor, or contractor-delivered is unknown.

rubric_notes: The verdict is: buy, gated on integration-contract terms and ML capability.
Must foreground the capability gap (no named ML engineer) as the risk that sizes the upside.
Penalise versions that lead with company history or list all four lenses evenly.
